title: Agent Context Grows With Every Step
concept: Each tool call and observation adds to the context, compounding the budget problem across an agent loop.

exercises.prompt: Quick warm-up: In an AI agent's loop, what typically happens right after the agent calls a tool?

options.text: The agent receives an observation — the tool's result — which gets added to its context
The agent's context automatically resets to empty
The model gets retrained on the tool's output

exercises.explain: After a tool call, the agent receives an observation (the tool's output), and that observation is added into the context so the agent can use it for its next decision. This is the basic building block of an agent loop, and it's exactly what causes context to grow over time.

cards.title: The Agent That Ate Its Own Memory
cards.body: An AI agent starts a task with a clean, empty context. Ten tool calls later, it's stuffed so full of search results and logs that it forgets the original question. What happened?

cards.title: Context Grows Every Loop
cards.body: An agent works in a loop: it thinks, calls a tool, gets an observation (the tool's result), then thinks again. Each of those pieces — the thought, the tool call, and the observation — gets added to the context. Nothing disappears on its own.

cards.title: Packing for a Trip That Never Ends
cards.body: Imagine packing a suitcase, but every stop on your trip you add a souvenir and never take anything out. By day 10 you're dragging a mountain of stuff just to reach the next town. An agent's context window is that suitcase, and every tool call is a new souvenir.

cards.title: A Research Agent in Action
cards.body: Step 1: agent searches the web, gets back 500 words of results. Step 2: it searches again, gets 600 more words. Step 3: it reads a webpage, gets 2,000 words. By step 3, the context already holds over 3,000 words just from tool outputs — before the agent has even answered the question.

cards.title: Myth: Tool Results Are 'Free'
cards.body: It's tempting to think only the prompt costs context space. But every single tool call PLUS its full observation gets added too. A handful of tool calls can use up more context than the entire original conversation.

cards.title: One-Shot Prompt vs. Agent Loop
cards.body: A single prompt-response uses context once. An agent loop uses it again and again, stacking up with every step.

left.text: One question, one answer — context used once, then done.

right.text: Think, call tool, observe, repeat — context keeps growing every single step.

exercises.prompt: Why does an agent's context problem get worse the longer its loop runs?

options.text: Because the model's weights change during the loop
Because each step adds new tool calls and observations on top of everything already there, so the total keeps compounding
Because longer loops always trigger more errors from the tools
Because the agent forgets its original instructions after step 1

exercises.explain: Nothing is removed automatically — every new tool call and its observation stacks on top of the previous context. That's why the problem compounds: step 10's context includes everything from steps 1 through 9, plus new material.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In an agent loop, only the agent's own 'thoughts' get added to context — tool call results are handled separately and don't count against the budget.
exercises.explain: False. Both the tool call AND its observation (the result) get added to the same context. There's no separate free space — everything shares one growing budget.

exercises.sentence: Each tool call and its observation gets added to the context, which is why agent loops face a compounding budget problem.
exercises.explain: The 'observation' is the result the tool returns after being called. Along with the tool call itself, it gets appended to context — and since this happens every step, the context budget problem compounds over time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three explanations of agent context growth sounds AI-generated (overly generic, buzzword-stuffed, lacking a concrete example)?

options.text: As an agent iteratively engages with its environment, it dynamically accumulates contextual information through successive tool invocations, thereby progressively augmenting its operative context window in a compounding manner.
Every time the agent calls a tool, both the call and the result get tacked onto the context — do that 10 times and the context balloons fast.
Think of the agent's context like a notebook that never gets erased — each tool call writes a new page, and the notebook keeps getting heavier to carry.

exercises.explain: Option A is stuffed with vague, fancy phrases ('dynamically accumulates,' 'progressively augmenting,' 'operative context window') without ever giving a real example or clear mechanism — a classic sign of generic AI filler text. The other two use plain language and concrete comparisons.

exercises.prompt: An agent is solving a task using a loop: think, call a tool, read the result, repeat. After 15 steps, the agent's context is almost full and it starts giving worse answers. What is the ROOT cause of this, and what does it tell us about designing agent loops?

options.text: The root cause is a bad initial prompt, so agent loops should never use system prompts
The root cause is that every tool call and its observation gets added to context, so loops must actively manage context size (trim, summarize, or select) or performance degrades
The root cause is the model's training data being outdated, unrelated to the loop's design
The root cause is that tools return errors, so the agent should stop using tools after step 5

exercises.explain: Each step in an agent loop appends both the tool call and its observation (result) to the growing context. Over many steps this compounds — the context window fills up, budget shrinks, and the model has less room to reason well or even loses track of earlier important details. This is why real agent systems need a deliberate strategy (summarizing old steps, dropping irrelevant observations, or compressing tool outputs) instead of just letting context grow forever. Recognizing this compounding growth is the first step toward context engineering for agents.
